arrays.title: Arrays
arrays.explanation: Arrays store items in contiguous memory, which makes indexing fast and predictable.
arrays.analogy: Think of an array like seats in a movie hall. Each seat has a fixed number, so you can go straight to the exact spot.
takeaways: Common foundation for most DSA problems.

linked-lists.title: Linked Lists
linked-lists.explanation: Linked lists chain nodes together using references instead of storing items next to each other.
linked-lists.analogy: Imagine stepping stones where each stone points you to the next one instead of all stones sitting in a row.
takeaways: Insertions can be efficient when you already know the spot.

recursion.title: Recursion
recursion.explanation: Recursion solves a problem by calling the same function on smaller inputs until a base case is reached.
recursion.analogy: Like nesting dolls, each smaller version gets opened until you reach the last one.
takeaways: Small subproblems are the key to recursive thinking.

prefix-sum.title: Prefix Sum
prefix-sum.explanation: Prefix sums store running totals so range queries become fast and reliable.
prefix-sum.analogy: It is like reading a meter at checkpoints instead of recounting every step from the beginning.
takeaways: Build the prefix array in one pass.
Answer range sum queries in O(1).
Pairs well with sliding window problems.

dynamic-programming.title: Dynamic Programming
dynamic-programming.explanation: Dynamic programming stores answers to subproblems so repeated work is avoided.
dynamic-programming.analogy: It is like saving a recipe step after step so you do not have to restart from scratch every time.
takeaways: Memoization and tabulation are both useful.

graphs.title: Graphs
graphs.explanation: Graphs model entities and relationships, which makes them ideal for networks, paths, and dependencies.
graphs.analogy: Think of city maps with intersections and roads connecting them.

example.title: Adjacency list
example.snippet:
A: B, C
B: D
C: D

takeaways: Represent networks with nodes and edges.

jsx.title: JSX
jsx.explanation: JSX lets you describe UI with HTML-like syntax inside JavaScript.
jsx.analogy: It is like sketching the page layout in the same notebook where the logic lives.
takeaways: Use curly braces for dynamic expressions.

components.title: Components
components.explanation: Components are reusable pieces of UI and logic that keep large apps maintainable.
components.analogy: Like Lego blocks, components can be assembled into bigger structures.

state.title: State
state.explanation: State stores changing values that drive interface updates.
state.analogy: It is like a dashboard gauge that updates whenever new information arrives.
takeaways: Keep state as local as possible.
Derive UI from data instead of manual DOM updates.

hooks.title: Hooks
hooks.explanation: Hooks connect React features like state and lifecycle to function components.
hooks.analogy: Think of hooks as plug-in ports that attach behavior to a component.
takeaways: Use them to keep components lean.

regression.title: Regression
regression.explanation: Regression predicts continuous values like prices or temperatures.
regression.analogy: Like estimating home value from size, location, and condition.

classification.title: Classification
classification.explanation: Classification assigns input examples to discrete categories.
classification.analogy: Like sorting emails into spam and not-spam.
takeaways: Focus on precision, recall, and F1.

overfitting.title: Overfitting
overfitting.explanation: Overfitting happens when a model learns the training data too well and struggles to generalize.
overfitting.analogy: It is like memorizing answers without understanding the subject.

scalability.title: Scalability
scalability.explanation: Scalability is the ability of a system to handle growth without breaking down.
scalability.analogy: A store adding more checkout counters when customers increase.

load-balancing.title: Load Balancing
load-balancing.explanation: Load balancing spreads traffic across multiple servers so no single instance becomes overloaded.
load-balancing.analogy: Like a traffic controller directing cars to less crowded lanes.

example.title: Distribution
example.snippet: client -> balancer -> server A / server B

caching.title: Caching
caching.explanation: Caching stores frequently used data so future access is faster.
caching.analogy: Like keeping frequently used tools on your desk instead of walking to the storeroom every time.

example.title: Cache flow
example.snippet: request -> cache hit / cache miss -> data source

takeaways: Cache invalidation is a key trade-off.

consistency.title: Consistency
consistency.explanation: Consistency describes how system replicas agree on the same data at a point in time.
consistency.analogy: Like making sure every branch office sees the same updated price list.
takeaways: There are trade-offs with latency and availability.
Distributed systems need clear consistency rules.
